cities.name: Mardin
cities.tagline: The honey-stone city above the Mesopotamian plain — UNESCO architecture, Syriac Christianity, and the most underrated old town in Turkey.
cities.summary: Mardin's old town is a single sloping street of golden limestone mansions, monasteries, and minarets, all looking south across the plain into Syria. Stay in the Old City — the new town has nothing for you. Most boutique hotels are restored 200-year-old mansions with rooftop terraces.

areas.name: Mardin Old Town
areas.oneLiner: The honey-stone single-street town with the famous panoramic view.
areas.verdict: Pick this for atmospheric stone-mansion stays — UNESCO architecture without the crowds.

areas.name: Midyat
areas.oneLiner: Smaller stone town 1 hour east — silversmith bazaar and the Mor Gabriel monastery nearby.
areas.verdict: Pick this only with a car — silversmith bazaar, monastery access, almost no tourists.

hotels.name: Zinciriye Hotel
hotels.whyStay: Historic Mardin mansion with terrace overlooking the plain.

hotels.name: Maridin Hotel
hotels.whyStay: Boutique-feeling Mardin stay with traditional stonework and rooftop dinners.

hotels.name: Reyhani Kasri Hotel
hotels.whyStay: Restored 19th-century mansion in the historic core, friendly family-run service.

hotels.name: Mardius Tarihi Konak
hotels.whyStay: 700-year-old stone konak with a hammam and panoramic terrace — the most atmospheric splurge in the old city.

hotels.name: Artuklu Kervansarayi - Special Class
hotels.whyStay: 58 rooms inside a restored historical caravanserai in central Eski Mardin — bigger and easier on luggage than the steeper konaks.

hotels.name: Gazi Konagi Butik Hotel
hotels.whyStay: Stone-mansion boutique with a terrace looking straight onto Mardin Castle and the Mesopotamian plain — strong breakfast.

hotels.name: Stone Boutique Hotel
hotels.whyStay: 500-year-old Ottoman-period building turned simple boutique — the cheapest way to actually sleep inside the UNESCO old town.

faqs.a: Mardin Old Town is well-touristed by domestic travelers and broadly safe. Foreign-office guidance has eased substantially since 2018, but always check your government's current advisory before booking the southeast.
Two nights minimum: arrive afternoon, sunset terrace, full day for the old town + Deyrulzafaran monastery, leave morning of day three. Add a third night to add Midyat.

cities.name: Konya
cities.tagline: Home of Rumi and the whirling dervishes — Turkey's spiritual capital and most conservative big city.
cities.summary: Konya is a one-night pilgrimage stop for most travelers — the Mevlana Museum (Rumi's tomb) and an authentic Sema (whirling dervish) ceremony are the reasons to come. The city is more conservative than Istanbul/Cappadocia; dress modestly. Most international travelers stay near the museum or in the small modern hotel district.
bestFor: One-night stop on a longer Anatolia trip

areas.name: Konya Center (Mevlana District)
areas.oneLiner: Walking distance to Rumi's tomb, the central park, and the indoor sema venue.
cons: Limited nightlife by Turkish big-city standards
areas.verdict: Pick this for the Mevlana pilgrimage — one or two nights, then move on.

hotels.name: Hilton Garden Inn Konya
hotels.whyStay: Reliable brand 10-min walk from the Mevlana Museum, modern rooms, breakfast included.

hotels.name: Bayır Diamond Hotel
hotels.whyStay: Central Konya 5-star with pool and rooftop, well-reviewed for the price.

hotels.name: Dedeman Konya
hotels.whyStay: Long-running reliable Dedeman with good breakfast and conference-friendly rooms.

hotels.name: Hich Hotel Konya
hotels.whyStay: 5-minute walk to the Mevlana Museum, restored stone-and-wood interiors, 9.1 on Booking — the boutique pick for the pilgrimage.

hotels.name: Bera Mevlana Hotel - Special Category
hotels.whyStay: Sits next to the Mevlana Cultural Center where the Saturday sema happens — can't get closer for the dervish ceremony.

hotels.name: Rumi Hotel
hotels.whyStay: 6-minute walk to Rumi's tomb, rooftop terrace, free parking — the best-value central option for a one-night Konya stop.

hotels.name: Konya Dervish Hotel
hotels.whyStay: Small family-run boutique near the museum — owner personally arranges sema tickets and city guides.

faqs.a: Public sema ceremonies happen every Saturday evening at the Mevlana Cultural Center (free), with a major festival in early December (Şeb-i Arus, the 'wedding night' marking Rumi's death anniversary). Book hotels well ahead for December.
Yes — Konya is 3 hours by car from Cappadocia, and the route passes Sultanhanı caravanserai. Easy 1-night addition to a Cappadocia trip.

cities.name: Şanlıurfa
cities.tagline: The city next to Göbekli Tepe — humanity's oldest known temple, plus the legendary Pool of Sacred Fish.
cities.summary: Şanlıurfa (often just Urfa) is the gateway to Göbekli Tepe, the 11,000-year-old archaeological site that's rewriting human history. Stay in the old city near Balıklıgöl (Pool of Sacred Fish) for atmospheric mansion hotels. The new city has standard mid-range chains.

areas.name: Urfa Old City
areas.oneLiner: Historic core around Balıklıgöl — restored Ottoman houses, atmospheric old bazaars.
pros: Authentic Urfa cuisine at the door
areas.verdict: Pick this for the Göbekli Tepe trip — atmospheric base, walk to Balıklıgöl.

hotels.name: El Ruha Hotel
hotels.whyStay: Restored Ottoman mansion with courtyard pool, walking distance to everything.

hotels.name: Manici Kasrı
hotels.whyStay: Boutique 19th-century mansion turned hotel; intimate, atmospheric.

hotels.name: Hilton Garden Inn Şanlıurfa
hotels.whyStay: Reliable modern brand for travelers who want consistency over atmosphere.

faqs.q: How do I get to Göbekli Tepe?
faqs.a: 20-minute drive from central Urfa. Most hotels arrange a half-day driver for ~$30. The site visit takes 90 minutes; pair with the Şanlıurfa Archaeology Museum (the original carved stones live there).
Şanlıurfa is significantly inland from any current security concerns and well-touristed by archaeology travelers. Standard southeastern-Turkey caution applies; check your foreign-office advisory.

cities.name: Safranbolu
cities.tagline: UNESCO Ottoman town — half-timbered mansions, cobbled lanes, and the saffron the place is named after.
cities.summary: Safranbolu is the best-preserved Ottoman town in Turkey and a 3-hour drive from Ankara (or a long day-trip from Istanbul, but better as an overnight). The Old Town (Çarşı) is fully restored 19th-century mansions, most of which now operate as boutique hotels. Spend a night in one.
bestFor: Day trip from Istanbul or Ankara

areas.name: Çarşı (Old Town)
areas.oneLiner: The UNESCO core — every street is preserved Ottoman architecture.
areas.verdict: Pick this to sleep inside a UNESCO Ottoman village — restored konak hotels at every turn.

hotels.name: Cinci Han Hotel
hotels.whyStay: Restored 17th-century caravanserai. Sleeping inside an original Han is the experience.

hotels.name: Gül Evi Otel
hotels.whyStay: Beautiful Ottoman house turned boutique hotel — intimate, romantic, garden courtyard.

hotels.name: Bastoncu Pansiyon
hotels.whyStay: Family-run pension in a historic mansion at the heart of Çarşı; simple but charming.

faqs.q: Day trip from Istanbul or overnight?
faqs.a: Overnight. Day-tripping is technically possible (5+ hours each way) but you miss the magic — it's quietest before 9am and after 5pm when the day-trippers leave.
From Ankara: 3 hours by car or bus. From Istanbul: 5 hours by car, 6+ by bus. There's no airport; the closest is Ankara.
